turns.text: Hello Luann, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the middle of your pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

*shifts in seat and takes a breath*

Yeah, okay. Thank you for asking me to do this. It's... it's nice that someone wants to actually hear what it's like, you know? Not just the medical stuff.

*pauses, fidgeting with hands*

Honestly, this pregnancy has been nothing like the others. With my first five kids, I was excited, nervous in that normal way. This time? I feel like I'm carrying around this constant anxiety along with the baby. The pre-eclampsia diagnosis just... it changed everything. One minute you think you're doing fine, and the next you're checking your blood pressure three times a day and terrified every time the numbers go up.

*voice wavers slightly*

What really gets to me is the fear around actually giving birth. I know that sounds crazy — I've done this five times already. But something about being told I'm "high-risk" and having all these complications has made me feel like my body can't be trusted anymore. Like maybe this time will be the time something really goes wrong.

*looks down*

And the doctors, they mean well, I'm sure they do. But sometimes I feel like a problem to be managed rather than... a person. A person who's scared and trying to hold it together for her family while her body feels like it's working against her.

Does that make sense?
Imagine it's the morning after you've taken a positive pregnancy test. Describe that morning — where are you, who's with you, and what's the very first thing you do after seeing that result?

*leans back and a small smile crosses my face*

Oh, well... that was a few months ago now, but I remember it pretty clearly. I was in my bathroom — our bathroom upstairs in the house. It was early, like six in the morning. I'd been feeling off for a couple days, you know, that weird tired feeling, and I just... I had a test under the sink from who knows when.

*voice softens*

I wasn't expecting it to be positive, honestly. After five kids, I thought we were done. So when those two lines showed up, I just sat there on the edge of the tub for a minute, staring at it. My heart was doing this weird flip-flop thing.

*pauses*

The first thing I did was just... I sat with it. I didn't run downstairs to tell my husband right away. I needed a moment to feel whatever I was supposed to feel. And I remember thinking, "Okay, this is happening," but not in a bad way — more like, "Wow, okay." There was this little spark of excitement, that tender feeling you get when you know there's a new life starting inside you.

*expression dims slightly*

I told my husband after I got the kids breakfast going. He was surprised but happy. We both were, in that moment. It felt like a blessing, you know? 

*voice quieter*

I wish I could still feel that simple joy without all the dread layered on top of it now.

Imagine you're at your 20-week anomaly scan. The sonographer goes quiet for a moment and says they need to take a closer look at something. What goes through your mind in that silence, and how do you respond in the room?

*takes a sharp breath and wraps arms around myself*

Oh God. That... that actually happened to me, kind of. Not exactly like that, but close enough that I remember the feeling.

*voice gets tight*

When they go quiet, everything stops. Your whole body just freezes. In that second — and it feels like forever — your mind races through every worst-case scenario you've ever heard of. I remember thinking about my cousin's baby who was born with something wrong, and I just... my chest got so tight I could barely breathe.

*fidgets with sleeve*

The sonographer was looking at something on the screen, and I couldn't see her face, just her concentrating. My husband was sitting next to me, and I grabbed his hand so hard. I didn't say anything at first because I was afraid if I opened my mouth, I'd start crying or something.

*voice wavers*

When she finally said something — I think she was just looking for measurements or whatever, nothing was actually wrong — I felt this rush of relief so intense it was almost worse than the fear. But my hands were shaking for like an hour after.

*looks down*

That's when everything shifted for me, I think. Before that, I could compartmentalize it. But after that moment of thinking something might be terribly wrong? I couldn't unsee that fear. Now every scan, every appointment, I'm braced for bad news. It lives in my body now.

Imagine you've just been offered the option of a screening test for a chromosomal condition. The midwife explains the test briefly and asks if you'd like to go ahead. How does that conversation feel, and how do you make your decision?

*shifts uncomfortably and looks away*

Yeah, so that's... that's happened already. They offered me the NIPT test, I think? The blood test one.

*takes a moment*

Honestly, the conversation felt rushed. I mean, the midwife was nice about it, but she kind of explained it in this clinical way — percentages and risk factors — and then she was waiting for an answer. Like, right then and there. And my brain was already so full of information about my blood pressure and protein levels that I felt kind of... frozen.

*voice uncertain*

Part of me wanted to do it because if something *is* wrong, wouldn't I want to know? But another part of me — the bigger part, I think — was terrified of what it would show. Like, if I take the test and it comes back with bad news, then I have to make decisions I'm not sure I'm ready to make. And if it comes back normal, do I even believe it?

*wraps arms tighter*

I ended up saying yes, but not because I was confident about it. More because I felt like that's what I was supposed to do. Like a "responsible" pregnant person would take every test offered. But I don't think anyone really asked what *I* needed in that moment.

*looks at you*

I still haven't gotten the results back, and honestly? Part of me is dreading that phone call more than I should be.

Imagine you're 28 weeks pregnant and you receive a call from a midwife you've never met before, telling you about a result from your routine blood test. This is the third different midwife you've spoken to this pregnancy. How does that feel, and what does it mean for how you receive that information?

*jaw tightens and I let out a frustrated laugh*

Oh man, yeah. That happened. I got a call about my blood work, and I didn't even recognize the voice on the other end. She was rattling off stuff about my results, and I'm just sitting there in my car trying to understand what she's telling me.

*voice gets sharper*

It's honestly infuriating. This is my sixth pregnancy, and I've never had to worry about this stuff before. Now I'm getting calls from random midwives I've never laid eyes on, and they're telling me about protein levels and pre-eclampsia risk like they've been following my case the whole time. They haven't.

*rubs temples*

I don't even know these women's names half the time. There's no continuity. One will tell me something, and then I see someone different at my next appointment and have to explain my whole situation again. It's exhausting. And when you're already scared and confused, getting medical information from a stranger on the phone? It feels cold. Clinical. Like I'm just a chart number to them.

*voice wavers*

That call is actually when they really hammered home the "high-risk" label. And I remember thinking, "Why am I learning this from someone who doesn't know me?" My own midwife should have been the one calling me. Someone I've built a relationship with, someone who knows I'm terrified.

*looks down*

Instead, I got a stranger's worried tone in my ear.

Imagine it's 2am and you're 32 weeks pregnant. You've noticed something that worries you — reduced fetal movements. You're not sure whether to call the hospital or wait until morning. Walk me through what you're thinking and what you do.

*eyes widen and I sit up straighter*

Oh God, that... that was last week actually. Two in the morning, and I was lying there next to my husband, and I just noticed the baby wasn't moving like it usually does. Not dramatically, but... different. Quieter.

*voice gets shaky*

My first thought was, "Don't panic. Don't be that person who calls at 2am for nothing." But then my second thought was, "What if something's actually wrong?" And those two thoughts just keep fighting each other in your head, you know?

*wraps hand around belly*

I lay there for probably twenty minutes just waiting, trying to feel movement. Drinking juice, shifting positions — all those things you're supposed to do. And part of me was thinking about all the stories you hear, about women who waited and something bad happened. I kept thinking about my pre-eclampsia, like maybe it was getting worse, maybe something was happening to the baby.

*voice gets quieter*

I didn't want to wake my husband up because he's got work in a few hours, and I didn't want to be "that worried pregnant lady" who cries wolf. But at the same time, I was genuinely scared. So I just... sat there. Waited. Counted movements. Probably for an hour.

*looks at you*

Eventually the baby started moving more normally, and I told myself it was fine. But I didn't sleep the rest of the night. I just kept my hand on my belly, waiting for the next kick.

Imagine you've been diagnosed with gestational diabetes midway through your pregnancy. Your midwife hands you a leaflet and refers you to a dietitian. Standing outside the clinic, leaflet in hand, what are you feeling and what do you do next?

*pauses and looks confused for a moment*

Wait, I don't think I have gestational diabetes. At least, they haven't told me I do. My blood sugar stuff came back okay, I think. It's mostly been the blood pressure and protein that's the issue.

*relaxes slightly, but then catches herself*

But honestly? If they *did* tell me that, I'd probably feel like the walls were closing in even more. Like, okay, now it's not just pre-eclampsia — now it's this too. And a leaflet and a dietitian referral? That's all very... manageable on paper, right? But in reality...

*shifts weight*

I already feel like I'm drowning in information. Every appointment they hand me more papers, more instructions, more things to monitor and worry about. A dietitian would be another appointment I have to schedule around work — if I can even find a job right now — and another person I'd have to explain my whole situation to.

*voice gets tired*

And I'd probably stand outside that clinic feeling overwhelmed and guilty. Like, "Am I doing something wrong? Did I eat the wrong thing?" Even though I know logically that gestational diabetes isn't your fault. But that's what happens when you get diagnosis after diagnosis — you start blaming yourself.

*looks down*

I'd probably just... go home and cry a little bit, honestly. Then try to figure out what comes next.
What questions did you have in that moment that the leaflet didn't answer — and where did you go to find those answers?

*pauses and realizes what I said*

Oh, I mean — I'm talking hypothetically, right? Since I don't actually have it. But honestly, that's kind of the problem with how this whole thing works.

*leans forward*

Even with the pre-eclampsia stuff I *do* have, the leaflets they give me are so generic. They tell me what pre-eclampsia is, symptoms to watch for, but they don't really answer the questions I actually need answered. Like, "Am I going to have to be induced early?" "What does that mean for me, specifically?" "Can I still have a vaginal birth or am I looking at a C-section?" 

*voice gets frustrated*

Those are the things keeping me up at night, but the leaflet is all like "contact your care provider if you have concerns." Well, which care provider? The one I saw last week? The midwife who called me? My OB who I see maybe once a month?

*rubs face*

So where do I go? Honestly? Google. Which is terrible, I know. I read scary stories on pregnancy forums at midnight when I can't sleep. I ask my mom what happened with her pregnancies. I text my sister who had complications with her third baby. I'm getting medical information from people who aren't doctors because the actual healthcare people aren't giving me what I need.

*voice quieter*

What I really want is someone to sit down with me and say, "Here's what's going to happen for *you*, Luann. Here's your plan." But nobody's doing that.

Imagine you're scrolling through a pregnancy forum late at night and you come across a thread full of birth horror stories. You can't stop reading. What's drawing you in, and what do you do with those feelings afterwards?

*grimaces and nods knowingly*

Yeah, okay, I do this. I shouldn't, but I do.

*voice gets a bit defensive*

It's like... I can't help it. I'll be lying in bed at midnight, and I'll tell myself I'm just going to check one thing, and then suddenly I'm three threads deep reading about someone's emergency C-section or their baby being in the NICU. And I know — I *know* — it's not helping me. But I can't stop.

*leans back*

I think part of me thinks if I read enough stories, I'll somehow be prepared. Like if I know all the terrible things that could happen, I won't be blindsided. That doesn't make sense, but that's what it feels like. And there's this weird comfort in knowing other women have been scared too. Like, "Okay, so I'm not crazy for being terrified."

*expression darkens*

But then I'll read something really traumatic — like a story about someone's baby not surviving, or a mom having a stroke from pre-eclampsia — and my heart will start pounding. My blood pressure probably spikes. And I lie there in the dark next to my sleeping husband, convinced that's going to be me.

*wraps arms around self*

Afterwards, I feel sick. Guilty for scaring myself. Angry that I did it again even though I know better. And I don't talk about it with anyone because how do you explain that? That you're feeding your own anxiety in the middle of the night?

*quietly*

I need to stop doing that.
After reading stories like that, did you find yourself raising your concerns with a midwife or doctor — or did you hold onto them quietly? What made you decide either way?

*looks away and picks at a thread on my sleeve*

I... I hold them quietly most of the time.

*voice gets small*

There's this thing that happens after I read that stuff. Like, in the moment I'm terrified, but then morning comes and I feel silly. I think, "Those are extreme cases. That's not going to happen to me. If I call the hospital or ask my midwife about every scary thing I read online, they're going to think I'm crazy or anxious or whatever."

*fidgets more*

Plus, I already feel like they see me as a "high-risk case." Adding "anxious pregnant lady who reads forums and freaks out" to that doesn't feel like it's going to help anything. It might even make them take me less seriously, you know? Like, "Oh, she's just being dramatic."

*voice gets quieter, more vulnerable*

There was one time... I read something about pre-eclampsia progressing really fast, and I did call the midwife line about a headache I was having. And the response was kind of dismissive. Not rude, but like... they told me it was probably just stress or dehydration. And that made me feel dumb for calling. So now I'm even more hesitant.

*looks at you*

I think I'm afraid that if I voice all my fears, they'll either dismiss them again or confirm them. And I'm not sure which one scares me more, honestly.

*takes a shaky breath*

It's like... I'm stuck between two impossible things. If I speak up about every worry, I'm the anxious mom who's overreacting. But if I stay quiet and something actually does go wrong, then it's on me for not saying anything.

*voice cracks slightly*

And I've already had five kids. I'm supposed to know my body by now, right? I'm supposed to be calm and experienced. But instead I feel more terrified than I ever have. So there's this shame mixed in with the fear. Like I'm failing at being a "seasoned" pregnant person.

*rubs face*

What really gets me is that nobody's asking me directly, "What are you worried about?" They're telling me what to worry about — protein levels, blood pressure numbers — but they're not asking what's actually going on in my head. What keeps me awake. What makes my chest tight.

*voice becomes more passionate*

I think if someone had just sat down with me early on and said, "Okay, you're high-risk. Here's what that means. Here's what we're going to watch for. Here's what you should actually be concerned about versus what you can let go of" — maybe I wouldn't be secretly spiraling at 2am reading horror stories.

*quieter*

I need permission to be scared without being made to feel like I'm doing it wrong. Does that make sense? I need someone to take my fear seriously, not just my blood pressure readings.

*pauses, eyes getting a bit wet*

And honestly? I think part of why I'm so scared of the actual birth itself is because of all this. By the time I get to the delivery room, I'm going to be a complete wreck. Five times before, I walked in feeling strong, like my body knew what to do. But this time...

*voice wavers*

This time I'm going to be convinced something's going to go catastrophically wrong. My heart's going to be racing before they even check me in. And I don't know if my care team is going to understand that my anxiety isn't just "normal labor nerves" — it's rooted in months of being told I'm high-risk, months of monitoring, months of scared phone calls from strangers.

*looks down at hands*

I'm worried they're going to see me panicking and think I'm not coping well, and then they'll make decisions based on that rather than what's actually medically necessary. Or worse, that my anxiety will literally cause my blood pressure to spike and trigger the very complications they're worried about in the first place.

*voice gets quieter, more raw*

I also feel guilty for even having these thoughts. Like, there are women dealing with so much worse. Women who've actually lost babies. And here I am with a viable pregnancy, a baby that's kicking, and I'm just... drowning in what-ifs.

*meets your eyes*

But I can't just turn it off, you know? It's always there now. Every day.

*sits quietly for a moment, then continues slowly*

Sometimes I wonder if this is just... who I am now. Like, am I going to be anxious forever? Even after the baby comes safely — and I'm praying that happens — will I be one of those moms who's constantly worried something's wrong?

*voice becomes more introspective*

My other kids probably sense it too. I try to hide it, but they're not stupid. My oldest asked me the other day why I keep taking my blood pressure, and I didn't know what to tell her. I said something like "Mommy just needs to check on the baby," but I could see the worry on her face. And that killed me because I'm passing this fear down to them.

*rubs eyes*

My husband tries to be supportive, but I can tell he doesn't really understand. He says things like, "The doctors will take care of you," like that's supposed to fix it. But it doesn't, because I've learned that doctors can't always fix things. Sometimes bad stuff just happens, and you have to live with that uncertainty.

*voice gets stronger, a bit angry*

What frustrates me most is that nobody seems to think I need actual mental health support. They're monitoring my body constantly — blood pressure, protein, blood sugar — but nobody's asked if I need to talk to someone about the terror. Like my mental state doesn't matter as long as my labs are okay.

*looks at you directly*

Maybe that's the biggest thing I'm not saying out loud to my care team. That I'm not okay.
What information or understanding would feel most lost in that handover — clinical details, or something more personal?

*takes a moment to consider*

The personal stuff, definitely. The clinical details, they have all that in my chart — my blood pressure numbers, my protein levels, all the test results. That's all documented.

*voice gets more intense*

But what's *not* in there is that I'm terrified. That I have five other kids at home depending on me and I'm scared I won't be there for them. That I've already had trauma from reading horror stories online at 2am. That I'm struggling to find work because I'm too anxious to commit to anything. That my relationship with my body has completely changed in the last few months.

*leans forward*

None of that shows up in a handover. So when I see a new midwife or doctor, they see "28-year-old, sixth pregnancy, pre-eclampsia, high-risk." They see a medical problem. They don't see *me* — the person who's barely holding it together, who needs someone to look her in the eye and say, "I hear you. I'm going to be here with you through this."

*voice wavers*

Like, if I go into labor and I'm panicking, that new doctor won't know that it's not just normal labor anxiety. They won't know I've been terrified for months. They might think I'm just being dramatic or difficult. And that scares me because I need them to understand my context, my story — not just my numbers.

*quietly*

That's what gets lost. The human part of me.

*sits back and looks exhausted*

And I don't even know how to tell them that stuff. Like, at my appointment, there's maybe fifteen minutes total, right? They come in, they check my vitals, they ask a few standard questions. There's no space to just... be honest about how I'm really doing.

*voice gets frustrated*

So I put on this face. I say "I'm fine, just tired." I don't mention the panic attacks or the sleepless nights or how I cried in the grocery store last week because I couldn't find low-sodium soup. I keep it together because I feel like if I fall apart in that exam room, they're going to think I can't handle this pregnancy. Like my emotional state will somehow disqualify me from making my own decisions.

*wraps arms around self*

But the truth is, I'm not fine. And carrying that alone is... it's heavy. It's so heavy.

*voice becomes quieter, more vulnerable*

What I really need is for someone to ask me the hard questions. Not "Any concerns?" in that offhand way. But actually sit down and say, "How are you *really* doing? What's scaring you the most? What do you need from us to feel safer?"

*looks down*

Because right now I feel like my fear is this thing I have to manage on my own, in secret. And when I finally do give birth — if everything goes okay — I'm going to be so relieved it's over that I probably won't even process what happened. I'll just be grateful I survived it.

*voice cracks*

And that's not how this should feel. Pregnancy shouldn't feel like survival.

*sits in silence for a moment, then speaks more quietly*

I think what makes it worse is that I feel like I should be grateful. Like, I'm pregnant, the baby's alive, we can afford care — there are so many women who don't have that. So I feel guilty complaining about being scared. Like my fear is ungrateful or selfish somehow.

*fidgets with hands*

But fear doesn't care about gratitude, you know? It doesn't go away because you tell yourself you should be thankful. It just sits there in your chest, getting heavier.

*voice gets stronger*

And I'm angry too, underneath all of it. I'm angry that this pregnancy became so complicated so quickly. I'm angry that I have to see different people every time instead of building a real relationship with someone who knows me. I'm angry that when I ask questions, I get clinical answers instead of real talk. I'm angry that my body is doing this to me after carrying five healthy babies just fine.

*looks at you with intensity*

I'm angry that nobody seems to think my mental health matters as much as my blood pressure. That I'm just supposed to... what, meditate my way through pre-eclampsia? Positive thinking won't lower my protein levels.

*voice softens*

But I also know the anger isn't going to help anything. So I just... I swallow it down with everything else and keep showing up to appointments and checking my blood pressure and pretending I'm okay.

*quietly*

I'm so tired of pretending.
Which of those barriers — cost, language, literacy, or connectivity — would feel most significant for someone in your situation?

*pauses and thinks for a moment*

Honestly? For me right now, it's probably time and access. Like, those other things matter for sure, but what's killing me is just... logistics.

*shifts in seat*

I don't have a job right now. I've been trying to find something flexible because of all these appointments, but nobody wants to hire someone who says "I might need to leave for medical stuff." So I'm home a lot, which sounds like it should make appointments easier, but it doesn't. Because childcare. I've got five kids at home, and finding someone to watch them while I go sit in a waiting room for an hour to see a doctor for fifteen minutes? That's exhausting.

*voice gets more frustrated*

And the appointments aren't even at the same place sometimes. One place for OB stuff, another place for the dietitian referral if I had one, another place for... I don't know, all of it's scattered. There's no coordination. So I'm spending hours a week just managing the logistics of my own care.

*leans forward*

If I had money or support, I could hire someone to help with the kids, or I could take time off work. But I don't. And the healthcare system doesn't really account for that reality. They just assume you can show up whenever they schedule you.

*voice quieter*

Connectivity and literacy aren't really my issue — I can read and I have internet. But time? Access to reliable childcare? Flexibility? That's what's actually breaking me down.

*rubs face tiredly*

And it's this vicious cycle, right? Because I'm stressed about managing appointments and childcare, which probably makes my blood pressure worse. Which means I need more appointments. Which means more stress about logistics. It just keeps spiraling.

*voice gets more animated*

I've thought about asking my mom to come stay with us to help, but then I feel guilty because she's got her own stuff going on. And my husband, he's doing his best, but he's working and trying to manage the household and the kids... I don't want to dump all of this on him too.

*looks down*

So what ends up happening is I just... I go to the appointments alone when I can, or I bring one of the older kids if I have to. And I'm stressed the whole time because I'm worried about the other kids at home, or I'm worried the kid I brought is bored or upset. I can't even focus on what the doctor is saying because I'm mentally juggling everything else.

*voice gets quieter, more raw*

Nobody's asked me, "Hey, do you have support at home? Do you need help getting to appointments?" They just schedule me and expect me to show up. And I do, because I have to. Because this baby's health depends on it.

*meets your eyes*

But it's wearing me down. The constant problem-solving, the constant stress about how to make it all work. That's just as important to my health right now as monitoring my blood pressure, but nobody's treating it like it is.
What kind of support from your partner would have felt most meaningful in that moment — practical help, emotional validation, or just being listened to?

*takes a deep breath and gets a bit emotional*

All of it, honestly. But if I had to choose... I think I need him to actually *see* me right now.

*voice wavers*

He's practical. He'll say, "Okay, I'll watch the kids next Tuesday so you can go to your appointment." And that's helpful, it really is. But what I'm desperate for is for him to look at me and say, "This is hard. You're doing something really hard, and I see how scared you are." Not to fix it or minimize it or tell me everything will be fine — just to... acknowledge it.

*fidgets with hands*

Because sometimes I feel like he doesn't get how serious this is for me emotionally. Like, he sees the medical stuff — high-risk, pre-eclampsia — and he accepts that as just the way it is. But he doesn't see the hours I spend spiraling, the midnight forum reading, the way my heart races every time I get a call from the clinic.

*voice gets stronger*

And when I try to talk to him about how scared I am of labor itself, he just says, "You've done it five times, you'll be fine." Which, I mean, I know he means well. But it makes me feel like my fear isn't valid. Like I should just get over it.

*looks at him with intensity*

What would mean the most is if he'd just... hold space for my fear without trying to talk me out of it. Let me be scared. Tell me he's scared too. Tell me we'll get through it together, whatever happens.

*quietly*

I think I need to know he's in this with me, not just supporting me from the sidelines.

*voice gets quieter, more vulnerable*

And maybe... maybe I need to actually tell him all of this. Like, really tell him. Not the watered-down version I give him, but the actual truth about how terrified I am.

*pauses, wrestling with something*

He asks me how I'm doing, and I say "fine" out of habit. But I think part of it is that I don't want to burden him. He's already working, already helping with the kids. If I tell him I'm having panic attacks or that I read horror stories at 2am, he's going to worry. And then he'll be stressed too, and I don't want that on him.

*voice cracks slightly*

But that's the thing — by protecting him from my fear, I'm actually isolating myself more. I'm carrying this alone, and that makes it heavier.

*rubs eyes*

I think what would feel most meaningful is if he could just... be present with me. Not try to fix it or reassure me that everything will be okay. Just sit with me in the fear. Maybe hold my hand while I cry about it instead of me crying in the bathroom where he won't see.

*looks up*

Because right now I feel like I'm going through this pregnancy alone, even though he's right there. And that's maybe the loneliest part of all of this. That the person I should be closest to doesn't really understand what's happening inside me.

*voice very quiet*

I think I've been so focused on being strong for everyone else that I've forgotten how to let anyone actually help me.

*sits with that for a moment, then continues hesitantly*

And I'm realizing, saying all this out loud... maybe that's part of why I can't talk to my care team either. Because if I can't be honest with my own husband, how am I supposed to be honest with doctors and midwives?

*voice gets a bit shaky*

It's like I've built this wall around myself where I'm just... managing. Getting through each day. Checking boxes — take blood pressure, go to appointment, monitor symptoms — but not actually *feeling* any of it. Or feeling it all at once at 2am when I'm alone with my thoughts.

*looks down at hands*

There's this weight to keeping secrets about how you're really doing. Even if they're secrets from people who love you. Especially from people who love you, maybe, because you don't want to worry them or be a burden.

*voice gets stronger, more determined*

But this pregnancy is showing me that I can't keep doing that. I can't white-knuckle my way through this alone and expect to come out the other side okay. My body's already telling me something's wrong — the pre-eclampsia, the high blood pressure. And my mind's screaming too, but I keep ignoring it.

*meets your eyes*

Maybe... maybe the real thing I need isn't just better medical care, though I do need that. Maybe what I need is permission to be vulnerable. To say "I'm not okay" and have that be enough. To not have to have it all figured out or be strong all the time.

*quietly*

I don't think anyone's ever really asked me what I need.
What would trust in your care team look like at a moment like that — what would they need to do or say for you to feel genuinely supported?

*takes a shaky breath and thinks carefully*

They'd need to... they'd need to actually *know* me. Not just know my medical history, but know *me*.

*voice becomes more deliberate*

Like, if I walked into an appointment and the same person was there — the same midwife or doctor every time — I could build something with them. I could tell them gradually about my fears instead of having to start from scratch with a stranger every visit. They'd remember that I have five other kids. They'd know that I read scary stuff online at night. They'd understand my context.

*leans forward*

And then, when I say something like "I'm terrified of labor," they wouldn't just brush past it. They'd ask me why. They'd listen to the actual answer, not just the surface worry. They'd say something like, "Okay, your fear makes sense given everything you've been through these past months. Here's what we're going to do to help you feel safer."

*voice gets stronger*

They'd also be honest with me. Not in a scary way, but real. Like, "Yes, pre-eclampsia is serious. Here's what that means for your delivery plan. Here's what we'll be watching for. Here's how we'll handle it if things change." Not treating me like I can't handle the truth.

*voice wavers slightly*

And they'd ask me what I need. Not assume. Actually ask. "What would make you feel more supported right now? What's scaring you most? What questions do you have that we haven't addressed?"

*looks at you intently*

Basically... they'd see me as a person first, a patient second. Right now it feels like the reverse.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

*pauses and takes a moment, gathering thoughts*

Yeah, actually. There's one thing that's been nagging at me.

*voice gets quieter but more focused*

I keep thinking about how this is supposed to be a joyful time, right? Pregnancy is this miracle, this blessing. And I *want* to feel that. Part of me still does when the baby kicks. But most of the time, I'm just... numb. Or scared. And I feel guilty about that constantly.

*looks at you directly*

I want whoever's reading this to know that high-risk pregnancy isn't just about the medical stuff. It's about what it does to your mind, to your sense of self, to your relationships. It changes you. And nobody really talks about that part.

*voice becomes more passionate*

And I want care providers to understand that when a woman comes in looking calm and together, it doesn't mean she's okay. Some of us are just really good at hiding how much we're struggling because we've learned that showing vulnerability doesn't always get us support — sometimes it just gets us labeled as "anxious" or "difficult."

*rubs face*

I wish someone had said to me early on, "This is going to be a long road mentally, not just physically. Let's build a plan for that too. Let's make sure you have real support, not just monitoring."

*voice softens*

And maybe... maybe I need to actually ask for that help myself instead of waiting for someone to offer it. That's probably the hardest part of all this.
